data.title: CAVA targets 64–68 new restaurants in 2025 while expanding loyalty and menu innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CAVA Group, Inc. (CAVA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brett Schulman stated that "we continued to demonstrate the strength of our category-defining brand, further cementing Mediterranean as the next major cultural cuisine category." He highlighted a 28.2% increase in CAVA revenue, 10.8% same restaurant sales growth driven by 7.5% traffic, and 15 net new restaurants, bringing the total to 382. He also noted surpassing $1 billion in revenue on a trailing 12-month basis as a company milestone and reaffirmed the company's intention to expand into new markets, including Detroit and Pittsburgh, with a long-term goal of at least 1,000 restaurants by 2032.
  • Schulman emphasized the rollout of new menu items under the Spice World campaign and the launch of National Pita Day, which drove "record high app traffic, and over 130% more rewards redeemed than originally anticipated." He also discussed the company's reimagined loyalty program, which has seen sales increase by 340 basis points as a percentage of total revenue since relaunch, with membership approaching 8 million.
  • Schulman outlined continued investments in operational improvements, such as the Connected Kitchen initiative, which includes a new Kitchen Display System now live in 42 restaurants, targeting 250 by year-end, and AI video technology assisting kitchen staff in four restaurants.
  • CFO Tricia Tolivar reported, "CAVA revenue in the first quarter of 2025 grew 28.2% year-over-year to $328.5 million." She noted same restaurant sales rose 10.8%, restaurant level profit reached $82.3 million or 25.1% of revenue, and net income was $25.7 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $44.9 million, up 34.6% year-over-year. She also highlighted $369.4 million in cash and investments with no debt outstanding.

Outlook

  • Tolivar provided full-year 2025 guidance of 64 to 68 net new restaurant openings, same restaurant sales growth of 6% to 8%, and restaurant level profit margin between 24.8% and 25.2%. Adjusted EBITDA is projected between $152 million and $159 million, with preopening costs of $14.5 million to $15.5 million. She stated, "Our guidance reflects both the evolving macroeconomic landscape and the strength we're seeing in our business."
  • Tolivar reiterated the expectation for same restaurant sales three-year stack to remain "strong in the high-30%s for the remainder of 2025."
  • No additional menu price increases are planned after a 1.7% adjustment in January.

Financial Results

  • CAVA reported $328.5 million in revenue, 10.8% same restaurant sales growth, and net income of $25.7 million for Q1 2025. Restaurant level profit was $82.3 million, or 25.1% of revenue.
  • Food, beverage, and packaging costs were 29.3% of revenue, an increase of 110 basis points year-over-year due to steak offerings. Labor and related costs were 25.7% of revenue, down 30 basis points, reflecting leverage from higher sales and a 3% investment in team member wages. Occupancy expenses improved to 7.4% of revenue.
  • Adjusted EBITDA reached $44.9 million, a 34.6% increase. Free cash flow for the quarter was $2.7 million. Equity-based compensation was $6.7 million, with a full-year expectation of $20 million to $22 million.
  • The company ended the quarter with $369.4 million in cash and investments and no debt.

Q&A

  • Sharon Zackfia, William Blair: Asked about loyalty program engagement and the upcoming tiered structure. Schulman responded, "We're really pleased with the performance we've seen to date... we're about 8 million loyalty members total to date and that's growing every day."
  • Unidentified Analyst, Bernstein: Asked about the impact of the Kitchen Display System (KDS) on throughput and store performance. Schulman noted, "the new kitchen display screen systems allow for much richer, deeper order management capabilities, including increased productivity." Tolivar explained that top-performing restaurants are spread across regions and formats, and lower quartile restaurants are generally newer.
  • Unidentified Analyst, JPMorgan: Inquired about market-specific performance and total addressable market. Schulman said, "We haven't seen any differences or market-specific weakness. We noted we've seen consistent performance across the country in all markets and in all regions."
  • Andy Barish, Jefferies: Asked about menu innovation and the impact of beef costs. Schulman confirmed another tentpole menu moment is expected later in 2025, likely a new protein, while Tolivar described the beef cost impact as "about 100 basis points is largely steak related and that in fact has also been contracted through the end of the year."
  • Allison Arfstrom, Piper Sandler: Asked about the chicken shawarma test. Schulman reported it's "testing in Dallas and Florida... pleased with the progress," and said it's a light operational lift.
  • Analyst, Barclays: Asked about restaurant margin sustainability and reinvestment. Tolivar highlighted investments in team member compensation and guest experience, noting menu price increases have lagged CPI.
  • Analyst, Stifel: Requested color on labor deployment model. Schulman described productivity enhancements and opportunities for further gains through adherence to deployment processes, with a focus on making "restaurants easier to run."
  • Sara Senatore, Bank of America: Pressed on demand environment and lunch/daypart trends. Tolivar reported, "nothing in our data suggests that our consumers' challenges... we have positive traffic across all geographies, all income strata, all formats and all dayparts."
  • David Tarantino, Baird: Clarified three-year stack comps guidance and asked about new market performance. Tolivar confirmed guidance reflects current business trends and noted strong openings in new markets.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on engagement drivers, innovation cadence, cost pressures, and margin sustainability, with a generally positive but probing tone. Several pressed for confirmation on guidance assumptions and implications of macro uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing operational discipline and strong consumer response. Schulman consistently highlighted strategic initiatives and robust performance, while Tolivar provided detailed, data-driven responses and reinforced the company's outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management sustained a positive outlook and confidence, while analysts continued to probe for risks and details on growth sustainability, but with less emphasis on macro headwinds this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • New restaurant opening guidance increased from 62–66 to 64–68 for 2025.
  • Same restaurant sales growth guidance remained at 6% to 8%.
  • Menu innovation focus grew with the launch of Spice World and more planned protein introductions, building on the prior quarter's pipeline.
  • The loyalty program's impact expanded, with sales through the program up 340 basis points and membership nearing 8 million, compared to a 230 basis point increase previously.
  • Management reiterated margin discipline and limited menu price increases, consistent with last quarter.
  • Analysts continued to focus on growth drivers, margin sustainability, and innovation pace, while management's tone remained confident and forward-focused.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased food, beverage, and packaging costs, particularly related to steak, were highlighted by Tolivar, "we continue to expect the approximate 100 basis point year-over-year comparative impact on food, beverage and packaging costs from steak to roll off by June."
  • Macro uncertainty was acknowledged in guidance language, but management stated, "our consumer remains resilient, and we believe that momentum is appropriately captured in our outlook."
  • Analysts raised questions on operational throughput, market saturation, and margin durability. Management responded by outlining ongoing investments in technology, team compensation, and supply chain mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

CAVA Group delivered strong top- and bottom-line growth in Q1 2025, driven by robust traffic, new restaurant openings, and expansion of its loyalty program. Management reaffirmed guidance for 64 to 68 new restaurants and 6% to 8% same restaurant sales growth, while emphasizing continued menu innovation and operational investments. Margin discipline remains a priority, and the company sees resilience in consumer demand across regions and income levels. Management remains confident in its long-term strategy and growth trajectory, supported by a solid balance sheet and ongoing focus on guest experience and team development.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Integra Resources targets Florida Canyon optimization and DeLamar feasibility study in 2025 while bolstering cash reserves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Integra Resources Corp. (ITRG) Q1 2025

Management View

  • George Salamis, President and CEO, emphasized that Q1 2025 represented Integra's first full quarter as a US gold producer following the Florida Canyon Mine acquisition: "In November, 2024, Integra transformed from a development stage company into a US gold producer through the acquisition of the Florida Canyon Mine. And in Q1 2025, Integra completed its first full quarter as a producing company." Salamis highlighted consistent production and cash flow, noting, "Florida Canyon is doing its job and performing as expected."
  • Salamis detailed Q1 gold production at Florida Canyon of 19,323 ounces at all-in sustaining costs of $2,342 per ounce, versus an average realized gold price of $2,888 per ounce. Operating cash flow was $16.1 million, and the period ended with a cash balance of $61.1 million.
  • Management reported three new executive appointments: Cliff Lafleur as COO, Sean Deissner as VP of Finance, and Dale Kerner as VP of Permitting.
  • Greg Robinson, GM, stated, "Gold production exceeded expectations partly due to the recovery and processing of approximately 2,000 ounces of previously unrecovered gold confined within an electrowinning tank as part of a one-time efficiency improvement project."
  • Dale Kerner, VP of Permitting, noted, "Integra submitted an updated and refined Mine Plan of Operations or MPO to the Bureau of Land Management for DeLamar. Submission of the updated MPO... initiates a pathway for the issuance of a Notice of Intent."
  • Cliff Lafleur, COO, explained, "Project has undergone some changes since the PFS, such as making it an oxide only heap leach -- an oxide heap leach only project and reducing site power requirements, all in an effort to streamline permitting."
  • Andree St-Germain, CFO, stated, "Integra closed Q1 with its strongest financial position to date, with a cash balance of $61.1 million and a robust working capital of $63.8 million."

Outlook

  • Management intends to provide 2025 guidance ahead of Q2 results. Salamis said, "We intend to provide 2025 guidance for the remainder of the year ahead of Q2 2025 results."
  • The company aims to complete the DeLamar feasibility study in the second half of 2025 and proceed with federal permitting.
  • Strategic priorities include optimizing Florida Canyon production, launching a 10,000-meter exploration drill program, and accelerating technical studies at Nevada North.

Financial Results

  • Florida Canyon produced 19,323 ounces of gold and sold 19,540 ounces at a cash cost of $2,016 per ounce and all-in sustaining costs of $2,342 per ounce.
  • Q1 revenues were $57 million with cost of sales of $41.5 million, resulting in $15.5 million in gross profits and a 27% operating profit margin.
  • Q1 adjusted earnings were $4.4 million, and adjusted earnings per share was $0.03.
  • The increase in profit margin compared to Q4 2024 was attributed to higher gold prices.
  • Sustaining capital expenditures for 2025 will include a $12 million South Heap Leach Pad expansion, set for Q2-Q3 2025.

Q&A

  • Heiko Ihle, H.C. Wainwright: Asked if the sharp increase in gold pricing has changed mining or exploration at Florida Canyon. Salamis responded that higher gold prices allow evaluation of historical dump material and other optimization initiatives, stating, "That's one of the ways where the improved gold price allows us to look at things like that."
  • Ihle followed up on cost inflation and capital expenditures. Robinson replied, "There's a few kind of smoking gun cost drivers that have temporarily elevated our mining costs a little bit in the form of capital pre-stripping... nothing really out there or completely unanticipated, but it is affecting us in the short term."
  • Phil Ker, Ventum Financial: Queried run-of-mine opportunities and crusher capacity. Robinson shared that the crusher outperformed, processing about 750,000 tons a month in Q1.
  • Ker asked about timing for mining equipment purchases. Salamis said, "The fleet will be kind of the top of the list of things that we'll be initiating in the second half."
  • Ingrid Rico, Stifel: Asked about lighter grades in Q1 production. Robinson explained that lower grades are temporary as they develop new pits, expecting better grades in the second half of the year.
  • Rico inquired about Nevada North permitting. Salamis noted that administrative delays are being addressed and increased cash from Florida Canyon allows accelerated de-risking and baseline studies at Nevada North in the second half of 2025.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost inflation, capital expenditures, and the impact of higher gold prices, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as management provided detailed explanations and reassured on short-term challenges.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing operational consistency, strong financials, and opportunities linked to higher gold prices. Salamis stated, "We are confident that Florida Canyon can continue to generate value for Integra for many years to come."
  • Compared to Q4 2024, both analysts and management demonstrated increased optimism, with analysts asking for more operational detail and management expressing readiness to deploy capital and pursue growth initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 is now anticipated ahead of Q2, while in Q4 2024 management refrained from specific forecasts.
  • Management added a third executive (VP of Finance) to the team since Q4.
  • Financial position strengthened, with cash balance rising from $52 million at end of 2024 to $61.1 million at end of Q1 2025.
  • Q1 saw the first full quarter of production post-Florida Canyon acquisition, compared to partial period in Q4.
  • Q1 profit margin improved to 27% from 18% in Q4, attributed to higher realized gold prices.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on cost drivers and capital deployment but sought more detail in Q1 on grade trends and permitting progress.
  • Management confidence increased, with clear timelines for feasibility studies and growth drilling.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited temporarily elevated mining costs due to capital pre-stripping, higher royalties, and tax-related production costs.
  • Ongoing component replacement maintenance is required for the mining fleet, with rentals being used to offset availability issues.
  • Permitting at Nevada North faces administrative delays, but additional cash resources are enabling de-risking activities.
  • Sustaining capital expenditures and mine optimization studies are in progress, subject to cost inflation and scheduling.

Final Takeaway

Integra Resources completed its first full quarter as a gold producer, delivering strong operational and financial results with Florida Canyon performing consistently and contributing significant cash flow. The company strengthened its leadership team and outlined key 2025 objectives, including Florida Canyon optimization, a major drill program, and advancing DeLamar’s feasibility study and permitting. Management emphasized operational discipline, capital strength, and a clear growth strategy as it targets becoming a mid-tier US gold producer.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Virgin Galactic targets Q1 2026 for new ticket sales as pre-revenue phase winds down
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Virgin Galactic Holdings, Inc. (SPCE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael Colglazier stated the company is making “solid progress toward putting our next-generation SpaceShips into commercial service” and reiterated expectations that the first research spaceflight will take place in summer 2026, with private astronaut flights following in fall 2026. Management underscored a continued focus on expense control during the pre-revenue phase and highlighted a balance sheet “with well over $0.5 billion in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities on hand.”
  • Colglazier detailed advances across rocket and propulsion systems, avionics, flight controls, and mechanical systems, noting that “our hybrid rocket motor system is a huge strategic advantage that supports the long service life of our SpaceShips while enabling quick turnaround time between flights.”
  • The CEO described “detailed program plans for each element of the ship,” emphasizing contingency planning and nimble execution, and announced a forthcoming behind-the-scenes series to showcase the SpaceShip build process starting in June 2025.
  • The company plans to reopen ticket sales in Q1 2026 with “a highly bespoke education and sales process,” anticipating price increases above the previous $600,000 per seat. Colglazier pointed out, “We expect the majority of people who will join in this first wave will be new customers, including both scientists and private systems,” while also highlighting repeat business from prior astronauts.
  • Virgin Galactic is midway through a feasibility assessment with the Italian government for a new Spaceport in Southern Italy. Colglazier noted “excellent potential in a Spaceport located in either Europe or the Middle East.”
  • The company is exploring expanded use cases for its carrier aircraft, reporting “initial feedback” from the Department of Defense and industry partners regarding potential government and research applications.
  • CFO Doug Ahrens stated, “Revenue was approximately $500,000 from future astronaut access fees and event fees. Total operating expenses for the first quarter decreased 21% to $89 million compared to $113 million in the prior year period.”

Outlook

  • Management guided for Q2 2025 revenue of approximately $400,000 for astronaut access fees and forecasted free cash flow in the range of negative $105 million to $115 million, indicating “lower spending going forward as the required peak investment level is now behind us.”
  • The company expects cash spending to “continue to decline through 2025” as it completes the current investment phase.
  • Colglazier reiterated that “we expect to open the first wave of spaceflight reservations in Q1 of 2026,” with pricing anticipated to increase from the last ticket price of $600,000.

Financial Results

  • Ahrens reported capital expenditures for the first quarter grew to $46 million compared to $13 million in the prior year period.
  • Adjusted EBITDA improved to negative $72 million in the first quarter compared to negative $87 million in the prior year period.
  • Free cash flow was negative $122 million, within the range of guidance. The company ended the quarter with $567 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities.
  • At quarter end, property, plant and equipment stood at $249 million, up from $209 million at the end of 2024.

Q&A

  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen: Asked about the total addressable market, free cash flow trajectory, and the impact of tariffs. Colglazier indicated the TAM analysis remains unchanged at 300,000 and expects strong early sales activity, with much of the needed material for the first two ships already in hand, minimizing tariff impact. Ahrens stated, “the trend through 2025 is a declining spend trend. And as you noted, yes, we're still targeting to be below 100 million spend by the fourth quarter of 2025.”
  • Chen also inquired about customer engagement and luxury positioning. Colglazier responded, “we are really excited as a company to start to make Virgin Galactic, a real name in that luxury and aspirational brand space.”
  • Greg Konrad, Jefferies: Questioned the size and cadence of the first sales wave and ideal backlog. Colglazier explained, “having a one to two year backlog is appropriate… we expect, in general, to be able to fly 125 flights over the course of the year.”
  • Konrad also asked about the Spaceport Italy feasibility study and funding. Colglazier outlined that airspace clarity is key and noted government support for the Italian region.
  • Gregory Dahlberg, Wolfe Research: Asked about repeat business assumptions. Colglazier shared, “we have, I'd say in the double-digits, low to mid-double-digits of people who have flown that have raised their hand and want to do a repeat with us,” but emphasized that referral business may be more meaningful.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on market size, free cash flow, backlog management, and expansion, with a generally positive but probing tone, particularly around financial discipline and sales strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently highlighting progress, adaptability, and the unique customer experience. Colglazier’s statements such as “we are really excited as a company to start to make Virgin Galactic, a real name in that luxury and aspirational brand space” signaled high confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from optimism about design and manufacturing progress to an emphasis on execution, cost control, and the approaching commercialization phase.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter placed greater focus on manufacturing execution, cost discipline, and concrete plans for ticket sales reopening in Q1 2026, compared to the prior quarter’s emphasis on design milestones and the start of assembly.
  • Guidance language shifted from outlining upcoming investments to signaling the peak of spending is past and a reduction in cash outflows is expected.
  • Analysts remained attentive to cash flow, sales cadence, and market expansion, with recurring questions about backlog management and sales timing.
  • Management’s confidence increased, especially around the transition from pre-revenue to commercial phase, and about the repeat business and referral potential.
  • Key metric changes included a year-over-year decrease in operating expenses and growth in capital expenditures as asset build-out accelerated.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management recognized the normal risk of delays in aerospace programs, citing a recent delay in wing part arrival and the need for replanning the assembly sequence. Colglazier stated, “we’ve been able to adjust our wing assembly order around the arrival of the late part. In this case, while wing assembly is now starting later than originally planned, the nimble adjustments from the team allow us to adapt to the change without impacting the critical path of the overall project.”
  • Contingency planning and schedule buffers were highlighted as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts expressed concerns regarding free cash flow burn, timing of sales reopening, and international expansion, but management addressed these as manageable within current plans.

Final Takeaway

Virgin Galactic management underscored substantial progress in advancing its new SpaceShips toward commercial readiness, with the first wave of ticket sales slated for Q1 2026 and an ongoing focus on cost management. The company expects to transition out of its pre-revenue phase as major capital investments wind down, supported by a strong cash position and a disciplined approach to manufacturing and commercial planning. Management remains optimistic about the demand outlook, repeat business, and the company’s positioning as a leader in luxury and experiential space travel.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Keith Meister's Corvex adds Sempra, exits Coke Europacific, among Q1 trades
data.description:
Keith Meister's Corvex Management took new stakes in Sempra (SRE)  and Heidrick & Struggles (HSII), while exiting Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP) and Femsa (FMX) in Q1, among other trades, according to its 13F filing.

The company added ~1.06M shares of Sempra (SRE) valued at $75.4M as of March 31, 2025; 1.00M share of Heidrick & Struggles ($42.9M), and ~2.62M shares of Integral Ad Sciences (IAS) ($21.1M).

It exited Coca-Cola Europacific (CCEP), shedding 799K shares; Femsa (FMX), divesting 405K shares and ~323K shares of Everus Construction Group (ECG).

The firm increased holdings in: Illumina (ILMN) to ~3.83M shares ($303.8M) from 1.70M shares, Dollar Tree (DLTR) to 3.21M shares ($241M) from ~2.89M shares; and Algonquin Power (AON) to 5.68M shares ($29.2M) from ~3.35M shares.

It reduced its shares in: iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) to ~197K shares ($9.21M) from ~1.18M shares, MDU Resources (MDU) to 5.87M shares ($99.3M) from ~7.11M shares; and Coca-Cola Femsa (KOF) to ~999K shares ($91.2M) from ~1.07M shares, according to its filing.

data.title: Soy futures slide on rumors of cuts to U.S. renewable fuel-blending volumes
data.description:
Soybean and soybean oil futures fell sharply Thursday on the Chicago Board of Trade as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency sent a proposed rule to the White House for review on the amount of biofuels oil refiners must blend into their fuel beginning in 2026.

The contracts tumbled following rumors that the EPA's proposed federal mandates for biomass-based diesel blending for 2026 would be set at 4.65B gallons, Reuters reported; previous reports had said a coalition of oil and biofuel groups had lobbied the EPA to propose a rule for biomass-based diesel blending for 2026 at 5.25B gallons.

Sparking the rumors were comments by EPA head Lee Zeldin in U.S. Senate testimony on Thursday that reviews of renewable volume obligations will extend over the upcoming months.

Zeldin's testimony hit the CBOT soy complex hard, with soybean oil futures limit-down by three cents per pound, or -5.7%, to 49.32 cents per pound.

"The delay, the cautious tone used by Zeldin, and rumors of less than expected adjustments have created a bit of worry in the space," John Stewart & Associates said, according to Dow Jones.

CBOT soybeans for July delivery (S_1:COM) finished -2.3% to $10.53 1/4 per bushel, while July wheat (W_1:COM) ended +1.4% to $5.32 1/4 per bushel, and July corn (C_1:COM) closed +0.6% to $4.48 1/4 per bushel.

ETFs: (SOYB), (WEAT), (CORN), (DBA), (MOO)

Potentially relevant stocks include Archer Daniels Midland (NYSE:ADM), Bunge (BG).

data.title: Appaloosa drops AMZN, MSFT, QCOM, and adds to NRG, GLW, and UNH, among other Q1 moves
data.description:
Appaloosa Management increased its position in Uber (UBER), and UnitedHealth (UNH), and cut its stake in technology mega-caps like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Qualcomm (QCOM), according to its 13F filings for the first quarter.

The American hedge fund, founded by David Tepper and Jack Walton, disclosed increases in positions in Uber (UBER) (by 113% to 3.2M shares); NRG Energy (NRG) (13% to 2M shares); Meta Class A (META) (by 12% to 550k shares); Corning (GLW) (by 16% to 1.7M shares); and UnitedHealth (UNH) (by 0.3% to 175k shares).

On the other hand, position decreases include Microsoft (MSFT) (by 47% to 510k shares); Oracle (ORCL) (by 50% to 700k shares); Vistra Corp. (VST) (by 14% to 2.3M shares); Amazon (AMZN) (3.5% to 2.5M shares); and Qualcomm (QCOM) (by 56% to 350k shares).

The hedge fund also established new positions in Deutsche Bank (DB) with 3.75M shares, L3Harris (LHX) with 300k shares, Broadcom (AVGO) with 130k shares, and Block Class A (XYZ) with 75k shares.

Lastly, Appaloosa exited positions in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), shedding 1.2M shares; FedEx (FDX), dropping 350k shares; EQT Corp. (EQT), losing 800k shares; and Antero Resources (AR), dropping 840k shares.

More on 13Fs:

  • Saudi wealth fund PIF boosts holdings in BE, AMZN; exits BLDP, among Q1 trades
  • Seth Klarman's Baupost adds FIS, SGI stakes, dumps SNRE, HUM, among Q1 moves
  • Jana Partners adds Freshpet, exits Wolfspeed, among other Q1 moves
  • Bridgewater Associates adds new Netflix, UAL stakes, exits 3M, Amgen, among other Q1 moves
  • Engaged Capital's Q1 moves include new stake in Yeti, GXO, exits PRA Group

data.title: TriSalus outlines at least 50% revenue growth for 2025 as new Medicare code expands addressable market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TriSalus Life Sciences, Inc. (TLSI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Mary Szela, President and CEO, reported "meaningful progress on its strategic priorities," highlighting growth in TriNav market share, advancement of the PEDD technology pipeline, and shifting nelitolimod to a partnership-focused strategy. Szela stated, "We achieved several key milestones in Q1 that laid a foundation for sustained momentum in 2025 and beyond."
  • The company delivered $9.2 million in net sales for Q1 2025, which Szela indicated was a 42% increase compared to Q1 2024 and an 11% sequential gain over Q4 2024.
  • Szela emphasized, "With the completion of Phase 1 trials for nelitolimod, we're shifting to a partnership-focused strategy. This transition allows us to eliminate all development expense on nelitolimod by the end of the year, yet preserve the long-term value of that program while concentrating our internal resources on the immediate and broader opportunity within our PEDD technology platform."
  • Key commercial developments included a 39% increase in unique ordering counts versus Q1 2024, the addition of 32 new accounts, and increased utilization per account.
  • The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services issued a HCPCS code (C8004) for TriNav mapping procedures, effectively doubling the reimbursable use of TriNav.
  • Recent launches include TriNav Large, TriGuide, and the upcoming TriNav Flex, with Szela noting, "TriNav Flex demonstrated to deliver improved trackability, and it is an important addition to our PEDD portfolio."
  • Szela also cited a successful $22 million private placement post-quarter, strengthening the balance sheet and supporting commercial expansion. The exchange offer converting preferred shares to common stock was completed with 55% of preferred shareholders.
  • James Young, CFO, stated, "Our revenue, solely driven by the success of the Trinav device in the U.S., reached $9.2 million. This sales achievement represents a 42% increase compared to the same period in 2024 and is also up 11% sequentially compared to the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • Szela reaffirmed guidance of "at least 50% revenue growth" for 2025, expressing confidence in momentum and expansion efforts. Management noted the company does "not anticipate being EBITDA positive or cash flow positive in 2025."
  • The company expects "a significant reduction in R&D spend, particularly in the second half of the year, and no further spend in 2026" related to nelitolimod.
  • Capital will be allocated to "accelerate development of new clinical applications of our core technology, as well as expanding our commercial organization," with the impact of added sales resources expected to be most significant in 2026.

Financial Results

  • TriSalus reported $9.2 million in Q1 2025 revenue, driven by TriNav device sales. Gross margin for Q1 2025 was 84%, down from 85% in Q1 2024, attributed to decreased factory volumes during a clean room expansion.
  • R&D expenses were $3.3 million, marking a 44% decrease from Q1 2024. General and administrative expenses totaled $5 million, up over 7% from Q4 2024, mainly due to audit and legal expenses.
  • Operating losses were $7.3 million compared to $11.7 million in Q1 2024. Adjusted EBITDA losses were $5.5 million versus $10.4 million in Q1 2024.
  • The company ended Q1 with $13 million in cash and cash equivalents, with an additional $22 million raised post-quarter.

Q&A

  • Justin Walsh, JonesTrading, asked about the PERIO 03 readout and partnership prospects for nelitolimod. Szela stated, "We have a device that works very well with very minimal side effects... about 50% of the patients are still alive, which is actually a good sign." Walsh followed up on interest in nelitolimod as a standalone asset or combined with TriNav, and Szela explained, "The way that we feel that really optimizes nelitolimod's performance is through our technology."
  • Ross Osborne, Cantor Fitzgerald, inquired about feedback on TriNav Large and clean room expansion. Richard Marshall, Medical Director, said, "Physicians are happy with it because they're able to treat a larger area... Its larger capacity allows physicians to deliver more drug faster." Young confirmed the clean room was completed in March and gross margins should "trend back up to kind of where they were previously."
  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, asked about sales force expansion and revenue cadence. Szela responded, "We plan to add sales resources starting now... you'll see the biggest impact in the fourth quarter. But really, the significant impact will be in 2026."
  • William Plovanic, Canaccord, asked about sales force targets and account numbers. Szela shared, "We have about 45 [sales reps and clinical specialists]... our goal is over the next 18 months, I'd like to get to about between 60 and 70."
  • Jason Witz, ROTH, asked if sales force expansion would achieve full penetration. Szela replied, "It’s roughly about 400 hospitals that represent about 80% of the procedural volume... over the next 18 months, that will get us there if we fill all of those reps."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was positive, focusing on growth, expansion, and product adoption. Multiple congratulations were expressed, and questions sought further details on commercial execution, new product adoption, and strategic direction.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during prepared remarks, frequently referencing "momentum," "meaningful progress," and "strong tailwinds." During Q&A, responses remained detailed and positive, with Szela emphasizing continued growth and opportunity, and Young providing reassurance regarding margin improvement.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management was consistently positive, with a slight increase in confidence—especially around reimbursement and commercial expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • 2025 Q1 guidance language remains focused on "at least 50% revenue growth," consistent with Q4 2024, but management now explicitly stated no expectation for EBITDA or cash flow positivity in 2025, a change from the previous quarter's positive full-year EBITDA and cash flow guidance.
  • Strategic focus has shifted further toward commercialization and expansion of PEDD applications, with nelitolimod development expenses winding down and a greater emphasis on partnerships.
  • Analysts continued to focus on sales force buildout, revenue cadence, and new account additions, with more attention this quarter on the impact of the new Medicare mapping code and sales force expansion timeline.
  • Management tone remains confident, with increased clarity on capital allocation and operational scaling. Analyst sentiment is slightly more positive, reflecting optimism about reimbursement and market opportunity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified no expectation for EBITDA or cash flow positivity in 2025, citing deliberate investment in commercial expansion and pipeline development.
  • Szela acknowledged, "We do not anticipate being EBITDA positive or cash flow positive in 2025."
  • The company is allocating capital toward new clinical applications and sales force expansion, which may delay profitability.
  • Gross margin was slightly lower due to clean room expansion, but Young expects improvement in upcoming quarters.

Final Takeaway

TriSalus Life Sciences enters the remainder of 2025 with strong momentum, supported by robust TriNav device sales growth, expanded reimbursement, and new product launches. Management reiterated at least 50% revenue growth guidance while emphasizing continued investment in commercial expansion and novel clinical applications. With the completion of nelitolimod Phase 1 trials and a shift to partnership strategy, the company expects to reduce R&D expenses and focus resources on scaling its PEDD platform, setting the stage for further commercial success and broader market penetration in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nuvve outlines $400M New Mexico opportunity and initiates Fermata acquisition as transformation continues
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nuvve Holding Corp. (NVVE) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gregory Poilasne described the quarter as "a good transition quarter," noting, "Though our top-line revenue for the quarter is not yet where we want, we are in a much better position than Q1 2024." He highlighted that Nuvve received 28 orders for a new charging station versus only one a year ago and reported a growing backlog. Poilasne emphasized a recent contract with the state of New Mexico, stating, "This project represents a potential opportunity greater than $400 million of CapEx deployment over the next four years." The company has established Nuvve New Mexico, with former COO Ted Smith named CEO of the new entity.
  • Poilasne also described international expansion: "Nuvve Japan is now established, and we have brought on board Masa Higashida to lead our operations in Japan. This new company is initially focused on stationary storage deployment monetization."
  • The company announced the acquisition of Fermata Energy assets into Fermata 2.0, with Poilasne explaining, "We have been able to execute this acquisition without any cash coming from the Nuvve holding balance sheet, while acquiring a mature pipeline allowing for extended long-term opportunities."
  • Additionally, Nuvve launched a new subsidiary for digital asset management, aiming to build "a diversified portfolio of cryptocurrency."
  • CFO David Robson stated, "In the first quarter, we generated total revenues of $0.9 million, compared to $0.8 million in the first quarter of 2024. The growth was primarily driven by increased charger hardware sales versus the same period last year."

Outlook

  • Management expects further growth in megawatts under management through the commissioning of customer backlog and anticipated new business for both EV chargers and stationary batteries.
  • Robson explained, "We continue to expect further growth in our megawatts under management 2025 as we continue to commission our backlog of customer orders we have earned, in addition to new business we anticipate winning, which we have visibility to in our pipeline."
  • Upcoming quarters are expected to show more developments on the New Mexico contract and the Fresno project, along with improvements in cash burn.

Financial Results

  • Robson reported, "Margins on products, services, and grant revenues were 39.9% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 34.7% for the year-ago period."
  • Operating costs, excluding cost of sales, were $6 million for Q1 2025, compared to $5.9 million for Q4 2024 and $7.5 million for Q1 2024.
  • Net loss attributable to common stockholders decreased in Q1 2025 to $6.9 million from $7 million in Q1 2024.
  • Cash operating expenses, excluding cost of sales, stock compensation, and depreciation and amortization, were $5.3 million in Q1 2025, compared to $6.6 million in Q1 2024.
  • Megawatts under management increased 3.6% over Q4 2024 to 31.8 MW, and backlog grew to $19.7 million as of March 31, 2025.
  • Cash position increased to $1.2 million at quarter-end, up $0.8 million from December 2024, primarily due to capital raised and net borrowings.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE: No analyst questions or management responses were recorded in the transcript provided for Q1 2025.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment cannot be assessed for Q1 2025 as there were no analyst questions included in the transcript.
  • Management's sentiment in prepared remarks was cautiously optimistic, emphasizing transformation and opportunity. Poilasne stated, "This quarter carries the fruit of many quarters of hard work to transform Nuvve, reducing our cash burn at the holding level, and focusing the organization on profitability."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone has shifted from defensive explanations regarding industry headwinds to a more proactive and opportunity-focused narrative centered on growth and transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter highlighted a transition to a drop-ship model and reflected a recovery in order flow versus Q1 2024, with 28 new charging station orders.
  • Strategic focus has shifted more aggressively toward new business models, M&A (Fermata acquisition), and international expansion (Japan subsidiary), compared to prior quarter's emphasis on cost cutting and survival.
  • Key improvements include higher gross margins, a reduction in cash operating expenses, and an improved cash position.
  • Management confidence increased, with forward-looking commentary on large contracts and new subsidiaries replacing last quarter’s focus on overcoming industry-wide delays.
  • Analysts' sentiment cannot be compared due to the absence of questions in the current call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited slower revenue recognition as the company transitions to a new drop-ship model.
  • Non-cash losses from the change in fair value of convertible debt and warrants, and increased interest expense, contributed to non-operating expenses.
  • The CEO acknowledged that "top-line revenue is still a work in progress," and highlighted the need to convert backlog into recognized revenue.
  • The company continues to focus on reducing cash burn and driving efficiencies to mitigate these challenges.

Final Takeaway

Nuvve’s Q1 2025 earnings call signaled a fundamental transformation marked by major contract wins, international expansion, a strategic acquisition, and a new digital asset initiative. Management stressed improved order momentum, an expanding backlog, and disciplined cost control. The company is positioning itself for significant growth opportunities, particularly with the $400 million New Mexico contract and the integration of Fermata assets, while maintaining a focus on reaching profitability through ongoing operational efficiencies and targeted investments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Vistra to buy 2,600 MW of natural gas assets in $1.9B deal
data.description:
Vistra (NYSE:VST) +2.5% post-market Thursday after saying it agreed to acquire seven natural gas generation facilities with a combined capacity of 2,600 MW from Lotus Infrastructure Partners for $1.9B, as it looks to meet growing power demand.

The deal includes five combined cycle gas turbine facilities and two combustion turbine facilities located across PJM, New England, New York and California, further geographically diversifying the company's natural gas fleet.

Vistra (NYSE:VST) said it expects the acquisition, priced at ~$743/kW, will be accretive to its ongoing operations adjusted free cash flow before growth per share from the first year following the closing.

The company also reiterated its expected return of capital to shareholders through an anticipated $300M in annual dividends and at least $1B of stock buybacks each year.

data.title: ESS Tech outlines energy base-driven growth and capital focus while pursuing 10+ hour storage projects
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ESS Tech, Inc. (GWH) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Interim CEO Kelly Goodman indicated the company’s focus this quarter was the execution of the energy base launch and gaining commercial momentum, noting, "Our focus this quarter was execution of the energy based launch and gaining commercial momentum... Our first quarter revenue is tied to final deliveries of our battery systems to our Florida utility customer and came in at $600,000 with roughly 65% tied to equipment and 35% tied to site preparation."
  • Goodman highlighted a major strategic shift, stating, "The results reflect in part our pivot from the Energy Warehouse and Energy Center products to implement a more focused business strategy related to the energy based product and address longer duration storage opportunities at 10 plus hours."
  • Goodman announced early traction for the new energy base product: "Within just three months of launching the energy based product, we secured early momentum. We were notified in late April that ESS beat more than 10 shortlisted competitors in a non-lithium RFP initiated by an Arizona public power utility... Contracting and approvals for the 50 megawatt hour, five megawatt pilot project are anticipated to conclude by September."
  • Goodman explained the company is exploring project-level capital and new revenue models: "Project level capital and monthly payment structures like PPAs or tolling agreements also have been new opportunities for ESS to maintain some level of ownership in project companies and receive revenue ratably that will help to smooth our revenue projections and provide a revenue baseline to the extent these projects close and become operational."
  • CFO Tony Rabb stated, "We reported GAAP revenue of $600,000 in the first quarter with the associated GAAP cost of revenue at $8.7 million... Our non-GAAP operating expenses for Q1 were $9.4 million and our R&D spend of $2.3 million reflects our investment in our cost out initiatives as well as the technology and product development improvements and performance, reliability and durability of the energy center as well as the energy based product."
  • Rabb explained, "We ended the first quarter with $12.8 million in cash and short term investments. Our cash burn rates in the first quarter reduced from the fourth quarter due to lower production rates, lower material purchases and proactive measures we took to reduce spend and more efficiently allocate resources across the organization."

Outlook

  • Goodman stated, "We expect that revenue will maintain these levels in the first half of the year and ramp in the back half based on energy based sales, although we are certainly exploring near term revenue opportunities."
  • Rabb confirmed, "We anticipate realizing revenue from our recent EB award once fully contracted and from other EV projects in 2026 and beyond."
  • Rabb indicated the company is "actively bidding projects to be delivered starting in ‘27 and ‘28 and beyond, where pricing expectations are trending towards 200 kilowatt hours or less on a fully installed cost basis."

Financial Results

  • GAAP revenue was reported at $600,000 for Q1, with a GAAP cost of revenue at $8.7 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was negative $15 million.
  • Rabb stated, "We monetized $1.9 million of our 2024 production tax credits in Q1 2025."
  • The company ended the quarter with $12.8 million in cash and short term investments, and management noted a reduced cash burn rate compared to Q4.

Q&A

  • Justin Clare, ROTH Capital Partners: Questioned Q2 sales outlook and the dependency of a ramp on successful capital raise. Rabb: "We do continue to moderate our spend in production until we can access the additional capital to support ramping production and orders."
  • Clare: Asked about cash runway duration and strategies to extend it. Rabb: "Our go forward cash burn will be lower than what we've seen in the past couple of quarters... we did launch our ATM at the end of the first quarter... Like the Exim loan that we have in place, and then there's a few other things that we've been exploring as well. But, obviously, critical for us to access additional capital to extend that runway."
  • Clare: Inquired about the specifics of the Arizona RFP win. Goodman: "It was non-lithium... there wasn't any particular duration, but I think the ability of the energy base to deliver at the 10 plus requirement is one of the things that helped us get over the edge... we were competitive on cost in this case."
  • Thomas Boys, TD Cowen: Asked about customer deposits and working capital. Rabb: "The ranges that we've seen historically are anywhere from 5% up to about 20%. And as we move forward on future contracts, our expectation is that we'll be looking to push that to the higher end of that range."
  • Boys: Asked about manufacturing capacity concerns. Goodman: "We have pretty significant additional manufacturing capacity at our facility... additional lines are relatively inexpensive from a CapEx perspective and also pretty quick to deploy."
  • Boys: Asked about status of the Australia project. Goodman: "The government funding for that project still has not come through. So we don't have any further update or visibility on timing for that project at this time."
  • Ben Kallo, Baird: Inquired about discussions with strategic partners. Rabb: "We are having a lot of ongoing discussions across multiple fronts with Honeywell and other existing investors."
  • Kallo: Follow-up on tariffs and inquiry levels. Goodman: "I would say to the positive... the tariff impact and uncertainty around the availability of imported batteries... has definitely driven inquiries in recent weeks."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was pressing and focused on liquidity, capital raise progress, and customer traction, with questions repeatedly returning to cash runway, capital solutions, and project pipeline risks.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic stance in prepared remarks, but responses to analyst questions frequently stressed the need for new capital and highlighted efforts to control cash burn, demonstrating a more defensive posture—"obviously, critical for us to access additional capital to extend that runway."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism persisted and management’s tone remained focused on operational discipline and capital needs, with slightly more emphasis on the positive momentum for the energy base product but still tempered by capital constraints.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company continued its strategic shift toward the energy base product, with Q1 highlighting early commercial wins and proposal activity, while Q4 focused on the initial launch and foundational deployments.
  • The emphasis in Q1 has shifted to tangible project wins (Arizona RFP), increased proposal activity, and the move to project-level capital structures (PPAs).
  • Management continued to stress cost reduction and capital discipline, but Q1 included more details on capital raising efforts and interim financing solutions.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters centered on liquidity, cash runway, and revenue trajectory, but Q1 included more direct queries about intermediate capital options and project execution risk.
  • Key metrics such as revenue and cash burn were lower in Q1, and management highlighted successful cost controls and a narrowing of adjusted EBITDA loss.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly highlighted the ongoing capital raise as a critical factor for near-term operations and future growth. Rabb: "We are aggressively pursuing all available options to extend our runway and maximize the value of what we believe is a critical technology in the broader energy landscape."
  • Uncertainty in the capital markets, tariff landscape, and legislative changes were cited as ongoing risks.
  • Delay in government funding for the Australia project remains unresolved.
  • Analysts voiced consistent concern on liquidity and the dependency of scaling production on new capital.

Final Takeaway

ESS Tech, Inc. is prioritizing the commercial rollout of its energy base product and has demonstrated early market traction with a notable RFP win in Arizona. Management is reallocating resources toward this new product and cost reduction initiatives, while actively pursuing a range of capital solutions to secure the company’s runway. The strategic shift is expected to drive a revenue ramp in the second half of 2025, but ongoing capital constraints and project funding delays remain significant challenges for near-term operations and growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
